Possessing two mutant alleles for LDLR , Apo[b] , PCSK9 , or LDLR adaptor protein gene locus 1 is considered a positive test for HoFH. Clinically, an LDL-C ≥400 mg/dL and one or both parents having clinically diagnosed FH, positive genetic testing for a known LDL-C–raising ( LDLR , PCSK9 , Apo[b] ) gene defect, or autosomal-recessive FH indicates a patient has HoFH. Document [9] (Title: Tissue Plasminogen Activator -- Mechanism of Action) Plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 (PAI 1) eventually terminates the catalytic activity of tPA by binding to it, and this inactive complex (PAI 1-bound tPA) is removed from the circulation by the liver via the scavenger receptor, LDL receptor-related protein 1 (LRRP1). In the nervous system, a neuronal-specific inhibitor of tPA, neuroserpin, acts similarly to PAI 1, and the LRRP1 internalizes the inactive tPA-neuroserpin complexes for removal from circulation. Document [10] (Title: Thrombolytic Therapy -- Indications -- Urokinase) Urokinase has been used most often for occluded catheters and peripheral vascular thrombus. It is considered a physiologic thrombolytic usually produced by renal parenchyma, thus purified from human urine. However, recombinant urokinase is also commercially available. In contrast to streptokinase, urokinase directly cleaves plasminogen into plasmin. Its low antigenicity allows repeated dosing without antigenic problems. [8]

Document [14] (Title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function) Fibrinolysis should be a consideration if the patient first presents to a non-PCI capable facility and the anticipated time from first medical contact to the performance of primary PCI exceeds 120 minutes (this 120-minute duration usually accounts for a 30-minute door in-door out time (DIDO) time goal at the non-PCI capable facility). Upon deciding about whether or not to use fibrinolysis, it should preferably occur within the first 30 minutes of the first medical contact. This strategy can be achieved either as prehospital fibrinolysis by the emergency medical service (EMS) provider or within 30 minutes of arriving at a non-PCI capable facility (door to needle time). Contraindications to fibrinolysis require prompt review if this strategy is an option. Absolute contraindications are any previous intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke in the past 3 months, central nervous system neoplasm or arteriovenous malformation, major head trauma or surgery within the past month, known active bleeding or bleeding diathesis (with the exception of menses), suspected aortic dissection, severe uncontrolled hypertension unresponsive to emergent medical therapy and use of streptokinase in the last 6 months. [7] Some relative contraindications include a history of chronic poorly controlled hypertension, surgery in the last 3 months, ischemic stroke more than 3 months ago, traumatic or prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), and any other unspecified intracranial pathology. Some factors merit consideration when considering fibrinolysis based on the available evidence from the recent clinical studies. Mortality reduction in patients with STEMI who are undergoing fibrinolysis is the highest within the first 4 hours, being around 80% after 2 hours and less than 20% after 4 hours of symptom onset. [13] Patients with developed collateral circulation may have more significant benefits beyond the 2-hour timeframe when compared to patients without collateral circulation. The CAPTIM trial compared prehospital fibrinolysis with PCI in patients with STEMI and found that patients who received treatment with pre-hospital fibrinolysis within 2 hours of symptoms onset had better survival when compared to PCI strategy. [14] Based on the STREAM trial, prehospital fibrinolysis provides similar efficacy when compared to PCI in patients with STEMI who presented within 3 hours and were unable to get PCI within 1 hour but with a higher rate of intracranial bleeding. [15] [16] An overview of nine randomized trials published by the Fibrinolytic Therapy Trialist collaborative group suggests that characteristics of patients who derive the least benefit from fibrinolysis are those with a delayed presentation (over 13 to 24 hours of symptom onset), elderly (age greater than 75 years), inferior ST elevations, and uncontrolled hypertension (systolic blood pressure (BP) more than 175 mmHg). [17] Thus, as a general framework, it seems that patients who present with early onset of symptoms but with a significant anticipated delay in transportation to a PCI-capable facility should get fibrinolytic treatment in the absence of absolute contraindications. While on the other hand, patients who present with onset of symptoms longer than 12 hours and absence of hemodynamic instability and evidence of ongoing ischemia of a large amount of myocardium should not receive fibrinolysis due to a likely lack of significant benefit. Other cases require evaluation on a case-by-case basis with careful consideration of risks and benefits. Upon deciding for a fibrinolytic strategy, agents that are fibrin-specific and given as a bolus (such as tenecteplase and reteplase) are preferred to other agents due to demonstrated lower risk of intracerebral bleeding and are more convenient to use. [18] [19]

Document [16] (Title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Development) FGF receptors are a family of RTKs that dimerize and autophosphorylate upon FGF binding. Document [24] (Title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action -- The Synaptic Cleft) The synaptic cleft is the space between the presynaptic and postsynaptic membranes, typically measuring about 50 nm across. Several biologically active substances interact in the cleft to promote and enhance nerve impulse transmission. These substances include acetylcholine esterase (AChE), lipoprotein receptor protein 4 (LRP4), and agrin. A glycoprotein binds LRP4 in the cleft, activating muscle-specific tyrosine kinase (MuSK), which helps differentiate acetylcholine receptors.
Document [25] (Title: Mural Thrombi -- Treatment / Management) Treatment of thrombi could reduce the risk of stroke, myocardial infarction, and pulmonary embolism. There are no standardized guidelines for the treatment of mural thrombi. Heparin and warfarin are often used to inhibit the initiation and propagation of existing thrombi. Heparin binds to and activates the enzyme inhibitor antithrombin 3, and warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, both enzymes are needed to produce clotting factors. Heparin is a preferred drug for dissolving the clot. If thrombi do not resolve after two weeks of heparin therapy, then surgery is an option. Thrombolytic therapy is another option for clot dissolution. It includes streptokinase, urokinase, reteplase, and tenecteplase. These are usually administered intravenously. Surgical candidates include younger patients, those having a low risk of perioperative complications, those in whom conservative treatment is unsuccessful, and those who have a highly mobile thrombus with high embolic risk. The surgical procedure includes thrombectomy, segmental aortic resection, thromboaspiration, and endoluminal stent-grafts. No approach is definitively superior. Endoluminal stent grafting is the least invasive option, but it carries a high risk of distal embolization through wire manipulation and stent deployment. [5] [6] [7] [8]

Document [10] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 760) Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass several interventional techniques used to restore patency of coronary vessels. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based method to dilate stenotic coronary arteries. Coronary thrombolysis is likewise a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and is considered a form of interventional cardiac catheterization; it serves as the parent concept for the broader procedure described as removal of coronary artery obstruction. The removal of coronary artery obstruction, which is linked to the Arabic term “رأف الوعاء,” is therefore understood as a specific application of coronary thrombolysis aimed at clearing occlusive material from the coronary lumen. Ptca is also associated with the term “رأف الوعاء” through a reference relationship, indicating that both ptca and removal of obstruction share the underlying goal of vessel reperfusion. Clinically, recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty can occur, highlighting the need for careful follow‑up and possible adjunctive therapies such as coronary thrombolysis or repeat obstruction removal. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchy and functional connections among catheter‑based coronary interventions, their therapeutic categories, and the potential post‑procedural complications that guide patient management.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 220) Fibrinolytics (fibrinolytika) are a therapeutic class of low‑molecular‑weight heparins and plasminogen activators used in fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis procedures that require contrast media. They are employed to dissolve thrombi in a wide range of vascular territories, including the femoral artery, intracranial arteries, lower‑limb arteries, upper‑limb arteries, the axillary vein, and central venous catheters, as well as in fluoroscopic angiography‑based thrombolysis of intracranial vessels. Specific agents identified within this class are SR 46349B, Y 20811, ardeparin, batroxobin, danaparoid, dazoxiben, sarpogrelate, semuloparin, uproleselan, and protein S (the latter as a child component of fibrinolytics). Additional related compounds linked to the class include sulotroban, kistrin and nafazatrom. The broader pharmacologic group “aktivátory plazminogenu” (plasminogen activators) encompasses fibrinolytics as a child subclass, emphasizing their mechanism of activating plasminogen to promote clot dissolution. Contrast agents (kontrastní látky), classified as indicators and reagents, are essential in each of these fluoroscopy‑guided procedures to visualize the vascular territory while fibrinolytic agents act on the thrombus. The integration of fibrinolytics with contrast media enables precise endovascular imaging and effective thrombus removal across multiple vascular sites, informing procedural planning, diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes in patient care.

These individual agents are classified as members of the broader group of antithrombotic enzymes, which also includes tenecteplase. Consequently, the various enzyme agents serve as constituent members of the antithrombotic enzyme class, highlighting their shared role in fibrinolysis and thrombus dissolution.

The therapeutic effect of these procedures relies on antithrombotic enzymes of the plasmin system; the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin is deoxyribonucleases/Plasmin derived from plasmin, and other key enzymes include urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, anistreplase, reteplase, streptokinase and tissue‑type plasminogen activator. All are child entities of the “Enzyme agent” category and members of the antithrombotic enzyme family. Antithrombotic enzymes are further classified within the tissue‑type plasminogen activator system and the tenektepláza (tenecteplase) subgroup,
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 75) “Other antithrombotic agents.” These antithrombotic classes are interrelated with fibrinolytics, and the broader term “Thrombolytica” links thrombolytic and fibrinolytic therapies. In diagnostic and interventional radiology, contrast substances classified as indicators and reagents are routinely combined with fibrinolytic therapy during fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis. Specific procedures include fluoroscopic angiography with contrast and thrombolysis of femoral, intracranial, lower‑limb and upper‑limb arteries; fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis of intracranial vessels with contrast; general fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis with contrast; percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein; and percutaneous thrombolysis of a central venous catheter. In each setting, fibrinolytics provide the active clot‑lysing effect while contrast agents enable precise delivery and real‑time monitoring. Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 190) of coronary artery obstruction by percutaneous transluminal balloon, single vessel” is a child of ptca and is described in Arabic as رأب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد (balloon dilation of the vessel through the skin). This removal procedure has a broader relationship with جراحة الشريان التاجي (coronary artery surgery) and is linked via “see_from” to the term رأب الوعاء. A clinical consequence of ptca is recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, highlighting a post‑procedural complication. Removal of coronary artery obstruction (general) has a narrower relationship with رأب الوعاء and a parent relationship to coronary thrombolysis, suggesting that thrombolytic techniques are a higher‑level category that also includes obstruction‑removal methods. Coronary thrombolysis, identified by the term coronary thrombolysis, is a pharmacologic reperfusion therapy that dissolves thrombus in coronary arteries. Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1982) Coronary thrombolysis is explicitly classified as a distinct therapeutic entity, and it is closely linked to interventional cardiac catheterization through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that coronary thrombolysis falls within the broader domain of interventional cardiac catheterization procedures. This connection highlights that pharmacologic dissolution of coronary thrombi is considered a component of the interventional cardiology toolkit, complementing mechanical techniques such as angioplasty and stenting. Recognizing coronary thrombolysis as part of interventional cardiac catheterization informs clinicians’ integrated approach to managing acute coronary syndromes, allowing coordinated use of drug‑based and catheter‑based strategies to restore coronary blood flow.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 186) Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) is classified as a coronary therapeutic service and a specific type of interventional cardiac catheterization that removes obstruction from a single coronary vessel by means of a transluminal balloon; it is also linked to the vascular‑repair term رأب الوعاء and may be followed by recurrent angina. Coronary therapeutic services encompass both PTCA and coronary thrombolysis, the latter representing a thrombolytic therapeutic role performed through injection into the coronary artery—a form of heart injection that falls under coronary artery surgery (جراحة الشريان التاجي). Coronary thrombolysis is a parent concept for “Removal of coronary artery obstruction,” which includes PTCA as a narrower intervention and connects to both surgical and endovascular contexts. Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 40) include fluoroscopic angiography with contrast and thrombolysis of femoral, intracranial, lower‑limb and upper‑limb arteries; fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis of intracranial vessels; general fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis with contrast; percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein; and percutaneous thrombolysis of central venous catheters. Thrombolytic drugs (thrombolytica) are listed among substances that can cause adverse effects, underscoring the need for careful monitoring; related safety concerns include drug‑induced poisoning by anthelmintics and adverse reactions associated with systemic antibiotics. Awareness of the interrelationship among fibrinolytic enzymes, heparin‑based antithrombotics, direct thrombin inhibitors, and plasmin‑activating agents, together with the procedural use of contrast agents, provides a comprehensive biochemical and clinical framework for optimizing patient care in the management of thrombotic disorders.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1734) Enzyme agent denotes a class of therapeutic enzymes that includes several specific fibrinolytic agents as its subordinate members. Within this group, urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, anistreplase, and reteplase are identified as child agents of the Enzyme agent category, reflecting their shared role as enzymatic agents used to activate plasminogen and promote clot dissolution in clinical practice.
